advaita.rendering: The destination attained by those who stand firm in knowledge (sāṃkhya-niṣṭhā) is the same reached by those who offer all action to Īśvara without desire for fruit — liberation (mokṣa) is one, not two. Śaṅkara insists: karma-yoga does not bypass jñāna but flows through it, purifying the antaḥkaraṇa (inner organ) until renunciation (saṃnyāsa) arises and direct knowledge terminates bondage. One who sees sāṃkhya and yoga as identical in fruit (phala-ekatva) sees truly; one who opposes them manufactures a division that the scripture does not support.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'परमार्थज्ञानसंन्यासप्राप्तिद्वारेण गम्यते' — both paths arrive through the gateway of supreme-truth-knowledge and renunciation.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: What the jñāna-niṣṭhas (sāṃkhyas) attain — direct vision of the self (ātmāvalokanarūpa-phala) in its inseparable relation to Bhagavān — is equally attained by those steadfast in karma-yoga. The two paths are optional routes (vaikalpika) to the same goal; choosing one does not forfeit the fruit of the other. The wise person (paṇḍita) is the one who perceives this single fruit and therefore neither inflates karma-yoga into a standalone mokṣa-route nor dismisses it as mere ritual obligation.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja: 'एकफलत्वेन एकं वैकल्पिकं सांख्यं योगं च यः पश्यति स पश्यति स एव पण्डितः'.

dvaita.rendering: Even yogins reach the fruit of knowledge (jñāna-phala) through the instrument of knowledge (jñāna-dvāra) — meaning no path bypasses the sovereign grace of Hari, who alone dispenses liberation to the jīva that depends wholly upon him. The verse closes the apparent contradiction raised in 5.4: both sāṃkhya and yoga are paths for the eternally distinct jīva, and their convergence in fruit demonstrates not metaphysical identity but Hari's uniform governance over all legitimate means.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: The place of liberation (mukti-sthāna) reached by those steadfast in sāṃkhya is identically reached by those steadfast in yoga who continue to act — because Kṛṣṇa bestows fruit autonomously (svātantryeṇa phala-dattva), not as a mechanical result of the path chosen. Both paths have one object (ekaviṣayatva); therefore one who sees sāṃkhya-yoga as one fruit (ekaPhala) is the right perceiver (samyag-darśana). In Puṣṭi-mārga this convergence is not philosophical abstraction but the direct expression of Kṛṣṇa's unconditional prasāda flowing through whatever form of loving engagement the soul undertakes.

bhakti.rendering: The place called liberation (mokṣa) is directly and fully attained (sākṣāt avāpyate) by renunciates firm in knowledge; karma-yogins reach that same place through the intermediary of knowledge (jñāna-dvāreṇa). Śrīdhara carefully notes the grammatical form 'yogaiḥ' carries a possessive suffix (mat-varthīya-ac pratyaya), signifying 'those who possess yoga,' not merely 'yoga as method' — ownership of the path matters. One who sees both as bearing one fruit (ekaPhala) sees truly; any perception of separate destinations is error.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'ज्ञानद्वारेण गम्यते' and grammatical annotation on 'योगैः' as mat-varthīya.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana reads a hidden temporal argument: jñāna-niṣṭhas attain liberation by removing the veil (āvaraṇa-abhāva) over what is eternally present (nitya-prāpta), while karma-yogins — through sattva-śuddhi (purification of the inner organ) — ripen into that same renunciation and knowledge either in this birth or a future one. The prior-birth karma-saṃskāras of any jñānī are evidence of past karma-yoga; conversely, present karma-yoga is evidence of forthcoming jñāna-niṣṭhā. Both paths interpenetrate across lifetimes as a single causal stream oriented toward Kṛṣṇa, whose form is the very content of that liberation.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'सत्त्वशुद्ध्या संन्यासपूर्वकश्रवणादिपुरःसरया ज्ञाननिष्ठया वर्तमाने भविष्यति वा जन्मनि संपत्स्यमानया तत्स्थानं गम्यते'.
advaita-bhakti.school_flag: bhāṣya present and most elaborate of the six

so_what_questions: If both paths reach the same liberation, what is the practical criterion for choosing between jñāna-mārga and karma-yoga in one's own life — temperament, circumstance, or grace?

Madhusūdana argues that present karma-yoga is evidence of future jñāna-niṣṭhā across births: does this make karma-yoga a necessary stage for everyone, or can radical viveka (discrimination) bypass it?
Śaṅkara insists mokṣa is nitya-prāpta (eternally attained) — only the veil is removed. If liberation is already present, what exactly does 'reaching a destination' mean in this verse?

Rāmānuja calls the two paths vaikalpika (optional alternatives) while Śaṅkara makes karma-yoga instrumental to jñāna. Does the verse text itself (yah paśyati sa paśyati) decide between these readings?

Madhva anchors both paths in jñāna-dvāra (through knowledge) — yet insists the jīva is eternally distinct. If knowledge cannot dissolve jīva-Brahman difference, what does the shared 'destination' actually consist in?

Vallabha introduces svātantryeṇa phala-dattva (fruit given by Kṛṣṇa's autonomy). Does this render the practitioner's choice of path spiritually irrelevant, or does the path shape the quality of the gift received?
The verse says 'one who sees them as one, truly sees' — is this a prescription (you should see them as one) or a description of what the liberated vision actually perceives?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you work without agenda — emptying the inbox, cleaning the kitchen, finishing the report — and notice the work doing itself without a 'me' claiming credit, you are practicing the karma-yoga that Śaṅkara says purifies the antaḥkaraṇa. The test is not whether you meditate but whether action leaves residue in the form of pride, grievance, or craving for acknowledgment.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: You do not need to abandon your professional role and retreat to contemplation in order to 'get serious' about liberation. Your diligent, caring, non-possessive engagement at work — offered as service to Bhagavān present in the persons you serve — is the same path as the renunciate's. Choosing either is valid; the Rāmānuja reading removes the guilt of being a householder.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In every deliberate act of worship — morning prayer, helping a colleague, feeding the hungry — remember that the outcome is Hari's to give, not yours to extract. Madhva's reading cuts the spiritual ego at the root: even your knowledge of the path is Hari's gift, not your achievement. Practice returns to gratitude faster.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's 'svātantryeṇa phala-dattva' means Kṛṣṇa is not waiting for you to perfect your technique before he responds. A devotee who sings badly, serves clumsily, or meditates distractedly is not disqualified — Kṛṣṇa's grace is not conditional on method-purity. This frees the practitioner from the anxiety of doing it 'right.'
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's grammatical note — 'those who possess yoga,' not just 'yoga as method' — points to an everyday truth: it is not enough to know the technique; you must have internalized it as a living orientation. Check whether devotional practice is something you do (technique) or something you are (possession). The former fluctuates with mood; the latter persists.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's multi-birth argument reframes failure: if today's karma-yoga practice feels incomplete or your knowledge feels thin, this is not evidence of unworthiness but of a process already underway across lifetimes. Present sincere action is the seed of future clarity. This reframe reduces spiritual despair while keeping the practitioner engaged rather than waiting for a dramatic conversion.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present with usable Sanskrit content; renderings draw directly from each commentator's distinctive move — Śaṅkara's gateway-of-renunciation, Rāmānuja's vaikalpika framing, Madhva's jñāna-dvāra, Vallabha's svātantryeṇa, Śrīdhara's grammatical precision, Madhusūdana's multi-birth causal chain — with real doctrinal divergence preserved and no bhāṣya absences to flag.
